mission.title: Install and Configure Porter on Kubernetes
mission.description: Porter enables you to package your application artifacts, client tools, configuration, and deployment logic into a single installer that can be easily distributed and executed. Installing Porter allows you to simplify your application deployment process using the Cloud Native Application Bundle Specification.

steps.title: Add the Porter Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Porter Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add porter https://charts.kubesphere.io/test
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Porter using Helm
steps.description:
Install Porter in your Kubernetes cluster using the following command. This will create a namespace called 'porter' if it doesn't already exist:
```bash
helm install porter porter/porter --version v1.4.0 --namespace porter --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the Porter pods are running successfully with the following command:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace porter
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits for Porter
steps.description:
To ensure that Porter has appropriate resource limits, you can edit the deployment with the following command:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment porter --namespace porter
```
Add resource limits under the containers section:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    cpu: 500m
    memory: 512Mi
  requests:
    cpu: 250m
    memory: 256Mi
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Porter will show the Porter pod running in the 'porter' namespace. You can also verify that the resource limits have been applied correctly by checking the deployment configuration.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add porter https://charts.kubesphere.io/test
helm repo update
helm install porter porter/porter --version v1.4.0 --namespace porter --create-namespace

resources:
  limits:
    cpu: 500m
    memory: 512Mi
  requests:
    cpu: 250m
    memory: 256Mi

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Porter release from your Kubernetes cluster using the following command. This will remove the deployment and associated resources created by Helm.
```bash
helm uninstall porter --namespace porter
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) that were created during the installation. Use the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete crd porters.getporter.io
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace porter
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the 'porter' namespace to clean up any remaining resources. Verify that the namespace has been removed with the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace porter
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current configuration and any important data. You can use the following command to export the current release configuration:
```bash
helm get values porter --namespace porter > porter-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the Porter release to the latest version with the following commands:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade porter porter/porter --version v1.5.0 --namespace porter
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check if the Porter pods are running successfully after the upgrade. Use the following command to verify:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace porter
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the Porter pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for more information. Use the following command to view the logs:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace porter
```
Replace `<pod-name>` with the name of the crashing pod. Look for any errors that indicate what might be causing the crash.

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails with 'release not found'
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter an error stating 'release not found' during the upgrade, ensure that the release name is correct and that it exists in the specified namespace. You can list the releases with:
```bash
helm list --namespace porter
```

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources error
troubleshooting.description:
If you see an error related to insufficient resources, check the resource requests and limits set for the Porter deployment. You can view the current deployment configuration with:
```bash
kubectl get deployment porter -o yaml --namespace porter
```

troubleshooting.title: Namespace still exists after uninstall
troubleshooting.description:
If the 'porter' namespace still exists after attempting to uninstall, ensure that all resources have been deleted. You can force delete the namespace with:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace porter --grace-period=0 --force
```

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster and the Helm and kubectl command-line tools installed on your local machine.
